agent_raw: The AutoML benchmark for this insurance claim investigation model has two critical methodological flaws that invalidate the reported advantage. First, preprocessing — including target encoding for high-cardinality categorical fields (repair shop, policy channel, assessor ID) and median imputation — was applied to the full two-year modeling table before cross-validation folds were created. Target encoding for a category computes the mean of the outcome variable within that category. When computed on the full dataset, the encoding for any category in a training fold implicitly contains outcome information from the validation fold records in that category. This is label leakage through the encoding step. The correct approach is to compute target encoding separately within each training fold, treating the fold boundaries as if they were train-test boundaries. Second, the final operating threshold was selected to achieve 80% recall on the held-out test set. Even though 80% recall is an operational requirement, using the test set to find the corresponding threshold makes the reported precision at that threshold an in-sample estimate rather than a truly held-out one. The threshold should be selected on validation data, with the test set reserved for final evaluation at the fixed threshold. Additionally, the comparison between the AutoML challenger and the logistic baseline is asymmetric: the challenger benefits from the leaky preprocessing and test-set threshold selection, while the baseline uses its original protocol. The steering committee should not accept this benchmark. A leakage-free rerun with matched protocols for both systems is required.
all_issues_raised: Target encoding fitted on full dataset before fold creation — label leakage
Threshold selected on test set — precision estimate contaminated
Asymmetric evaluation protocol between AutoML and baseline
